coredata.dc:title: Evaluating nitrite content changes in some Chinese home cooking with a newely-developed CDs diazotization spectrophotometry
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yong-Sheng
Zhao, Chun-Lin
Li, Bao-Lin
Gao, Xiu-Feng
coredata.dc:description: The research developed a diazo-coupling carbon-dots (CDs) method for determining nitrite and optimized variables of sodium sulfanilate, CDs synthesis, characteristic wavelength, reaction time and temperature. The method can assay 0.025–2.0 mg/L NO2 − and has a detection limit of 9.6 μg/L and 95–105% recovery. Subsequently, it was applied in detecting NO2 − changes in some Chinese home cooking, the gotten results indicated that if the sautéed vegetables (cabbage, Chinese cabbage, spinach and lettuce) and stir-fry pork, fried peas and pickled vegetable are stored at 4 °C for 72 h, nitrite contents are far lower than the recommended value, but if stored at room temperature for 24 h, the content in pure vegetables and shredded pork with green pepper will exceed the recommended value. Therefore, the staying fresher for the sautéed vegetables at room temperature is 24 h, if stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C, their staying fresher can be extended to 72 h.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon-dot synthesis
Nitrite
Ethylenediamine
Citric acid
Photometry
Chinese home cooking

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Spectrum scanning and TEM characterizing for CA/EDA-CDs
Effects of heating time and temperature in CDs synthesis
Effects of different amines on CDs synthesis
Effects of CDs formed by different EDA volumes on coupling reactions
Spectrum scanning and TEM characterizing for CA/EDA-CDs
Effects of common substances in samples on analyzing nitrite
Evaluations for reproducibility and linearity of CDs method
Determination of nitrite content in Chinese home cooking
Effects of temperature and time on nitrite content in leafy vegetables

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Examination of heating time in synthesis of CA/EDA-CDs (a) and effects on color products in NO2 −/SA/CA/EDA-CDs reaction system (b).
Influences of adding reagent order on azo chromogenic reaction.

Influences of different amines and ethanediamine volumes in preparating nanocarbon dots on azo chromogenic reaction. a. Effects of different amines; b. Effects of nanocarbon dots formed by different ethylenediamine contents. c. TEM characterizing and spectrum scanning for CA/EDA-CDs.
Standard curves for determining nitrite based on nitrogen coupling method of the nanocarbon dots.
Changes of nitrite contents with time in fried diced meat, fried peas, fried pakchoi and pickles.

Changes of nitrite content with time in four kinds of cook vegetables (Chinese cabbage, spinach, lettuce, green pepper pork). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Comparison of analytical methods for determining nitrite.

head.ce:title: Evaluating nitrite content changes in some Chinese home cooking with a newely-developed CDs diazotization spectrophotometry

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: West China School of Preclinical and Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: West China School of Preclinical and Forensic Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: West China School of Preclinical and Forensic Medicine, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The research developed a diazo-coupling carbon-dots (CDs) method for determining nitrite and optimized variables of sodium sulfanilate, CDs synthesis, characteristic wavelength, reaction time and temperature. The method can assay 0.025–2.0 mg/L NO2 − and has a detection limit of 9.6 μg/L and 95–105% recovery. Subsequently, it was applied in detecting NO2 − changes in some Chinese home cooking, the gotten results indicated that if the sautéed vegetables (cabbage, Chinese cabbage, spinach and lettuce) and stir-fry pork, fried peas and pickled vegetable are stored at 4 °C for 72 h, nitrite contents are far lower than the recommended value, but if stored at room temperature for 24 h, the content in pure vegetables and shredded pork with green pepper will exceed the recommended value. Therefore, the staying fresher for the sautéed vegetables at room temperature is 24 h, if stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C, their staying fresher can be extended to 72 h.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrite in foods can be converted into various nitrogen oxides when it enters the human body (Lundberg, Weitzberg, & Gladwin, 2008). For example, nitrite may be associated with secondary amines in the human body to form nitrosamine, which maybe induce cancer (Erkekoglu & Baydar, 2010; Robichova, Slamenova, Gabelova, Sedlak, & Jakubikova, 2004), but some medical experts explained that the incidence of cancer has little correlation with the intake of nitrite (Bryan, Alexander, Coughlin, Milkowski, & Boffetta, 2012; Habermeyer et al., 2015; Sindelar & Milkowski, 2012), arguing that nitrite could be converted into NO in the body, which has preventive and therapeutic effects on some diseases (Bryan et al., 2007; Gheibi et al., 2017). However, if nitrite intake is excessive, it will combine with ferrous ion in heme of hemoglobin to form hemiglobin that is unable to carry oxygen (Fan & Steinberg, 1996) and conduct the oxygenation when hemiglobin flowed through the alveolus, thus systemic hypoxia will occur (Bryan et al., 2007; Katabami, Hayakawa, & Gando, 2016). Therefore, China has given the recommended value of nitrite in different foods in food safety standards (GB-2762, 2017), and the World Health Organization also suggested to control the nitrite content in drinking water (<3mg/L) (WHO, 2017). Consequently, the quantitative analysis of nitrite in foods or water is still significant (Bulushi, Poole, Deeth, & Dykes, 2009). Because current mainstream thinking still holds that nitrite is a carcinogen, the main purpose of this study is to develop a method with suitable sensitivity to conveniently assay and evaluate the change rule of nitrite content in foods with the preservation time.

Presently, the latest methods for analyzing nitrite mainly include electrochemical, fluorescent, chemiluminescent, chromogenic methods and microfluidic paper-based analytical devices (μPADs) (Martinez, Phillips, Butte, & Whitesides, 2007; Morbioli, Mazzu-Nascimento, Stockton, & Carrilho, 2017).

The electroanalysis method is mainly based on the potential or current changes in nitrite being oxidized to nitrate. For example, Mani et al. modified the reduced graphene oxide (rGO) on a glass carbon electrode (GCE) to form an electrochemical sensor of rGO/GCE for sensing nitrite (Mani, Prakash, & Chen, 2012). Later, in order to further improve the resistance and capacitance of the electrode surface to perfect the electrical properties and detection sensitivity, Radhakrishnan et al. simultaneously modified rGO and Fe2O3 on the GCE to make the detection limit of nitrite lower 0.69 μg/L (Radhakrishnan, Krishnamoorthy, Sekar, Wilson, & Kim, 2014).

In addition to rGO/Fe2O3, the multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWNT) and its compound have also been used for modification of the electrodes. For example, Yang et al. used the synthesized porous three-dimensional nano-hybrid compound with MWNT/GO/hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) to conduct the modification on the surface of GCE, which was used to measure 0.20–37 mg/L nitrite (Yang & Li, 2014); Turdean et al. modified myoglobin and single-wall carbon nanotube on the surface of a graphite electrode to measure nitrite, in which Fe(II) of myoglobin was utilized to catalyze the nitrite oxidation (Turdean & Szabo, 2015); Haldorai et al. immobilized a layer compound that includes cytochrome-c (cyt-c)/titanium nitride/carbon nanotubes (WCNTs-TiN) on GCE to improve the sensitivity of the GCE on nitrite, in which the detection limit reached 0.064 μg/L (Haldorai et al., 2016). There are also similar reports in other researches (Ning, Zhang, & Zheng, 2014; Li et al., 2014).

Additionally, based on the resistance changes of nitrite initiating a diazo reaction and the further reaction with Ag-nanoparticles, Liu, et al. reported an electrochemical method of microfluidic paper-based Ag-μPAD to detect nitrite, in which the resistance of μPAD was detected with the four-point probe (keithley-model 2000) and the response signal was recorded by LabView system; the method has two response ranges in 0.01–5.0 μM and 10–320 μM with the detection limit of 6 nM (Liu, Hsu, Lu, Lin, & Ho, 2018).

In literatures related to the fluorescence, based on the mechanism of nitrite quenching the indole fluorescence in acidic solution, Jie et al. quantified nitrite of 10–600 μg/L (detection limit: 2.5 μg/L) (Jie, Yang, Jiang, Zhang, & Wei, 1999). Fernandez-Arguelles et al. analyzed NO2 − on the basis of fluorescence quenching of acridine yellow, in which the detection limit reached to 1.1 μg/L (Fernandez-Arguelles, Canabate, Costa-Fernandez, Pereiro, & Sanz-Medel, 2004). Based on bovine serum albumin-stabilized gold nanoclusters’s fluorescence quenching (BSA-AuNCs), Liu et al. tested nitrite in 0.90–2300 μg/L range, which had the detection limit of 0.046 μg/L (Liu, Yang, Abdel-Halim, & Zhu, 2013). Guo et al. reported a fluorescence enhancement method with the detection limit of 13.6 μg/L NO2 − (Guo, Zhang, Shangguang, & Zhen, 2013), where o-phenylenediamine was first reacted with nitrite to generate benzotriazole under acidic conditions, and then the formed benzotriazole was complexed with hydroxypropyl-β-cyclodextrin. Wang et al. reported another fluorescence enhancement method for nitrite, which was based on the reaction of dihydropyridine derivatives with protonized nitrite (Wang et al., 2016).

There are also some chemiluminescence methods in literatures for determining nitrite. For example, the use of nanocarbon dots (CDs) of glycerol/polyethylene glycol/serine to react with NO2 −/H2O2, Lin et al. determined nitrite of 4.6–460 μg/L, which had the detection limit of 2.4 μg/L (Lin, Xue, Chen, & Lin, 2011). To further improve the sensitivity, they added Na2CO3 to the reaction system, bringing the detection to lower 0.23 μg/L (Lin, Dou, Li, Ma, & Lin, 2015). Besides, based on CDs of citric acid/ethylenediamine (CA/EDA-CDs), the luminescence reaction to assay nitrite has also attracted researcher’s attention (Lim, Shen, & Gao, 2015; Song et al., 2015).

The nitrite assays based on Griess azo coloration have been most reported, in which the main goal is to find better reagents for diazotizing or coupling. For example, Sreekumar et al. utilized p-nitroaniline to replace traditional diazotization reagent of p-aminobenzulfonic acid (Sreekumar, Narayana, Hegde, Manjunatha, & Sarojini, 2003) and used ethyl acetoacetate or ethoxyethylenemaleic ester (Cherian & Narayana, 2006) as coupling reagent to detect nitrite; Noor et al. diazotized the safranine-O reagent with the protonized NO2 −, and formed the blue tetra-azotized compounds (Noor, Tan, Lee, Kwok, & Tajuddin, 2016) by self-couple, which was measured for nitrite. Nagaraj et al. used dapsone as a diazotization reagent to evaluate chromogenic effect of alpha-naphthol and 4-amino-5-hydroxynapthalene-2,7-disulphonic acid monosodium salt in nitrite determination (Nagaraj, Prakash, Shivakumar, & Shrestha, 2008). Diazogenic reagents such as cefixime, 1-naphthylamine (Shariati-Rad, Irandoust, & Mohammadi, 2015) and 2-amino-6-chlorobenzoic acid (ACBA) (Wang, Wan, Ma, & Wang, 2018) have also been successfully applied in the determination of nitrite. In order to improve the sensitivity of nitrite detection, the combination of the above diazotization reagents with new materials or new detection technologies has also been widely reported, in which dapsone with combined spot test/diffuse reflectance on filter paper was used for the determination of nitrite in meat products and water (Luiz, Pezza, & Pezza, 2012).

Additionally, the colorimetric assays of nitrite based on μPAD have been reported. Using color changes in μPAD sensing areas and an android program, Lopez-Ruiz, et al. was to determine nitrite, in which a mobile phone flash was used as the light source and a built-in camera was utilized to photograph the image of chromogenic area, based on the coordinates of HSV (Hue, saturation and Value) to quantify the nitrite concentration, the detection limit could reach 0.52 mg/L (Lopez-Ruiz et al., 2014). Subsequently, the use of an improved Griess reaction and μPAD method, Bhakta et al. quantified nitrite (detection limit: 10 mmol/L) in saliva (Bhakta, Borba, Taba, Garcia, & Carrilho, 2014). Using the stamping method, Cardoso et al. made another μPAD and determined nitrite, in which it was based on another improved Griess reaction (detection limit: 5.6 μM) and a scanner was adopted to capture images and convert them into color scales (Cardoso, Garcia, & Coltro, 2015). Ortiz-Gomez et al. also developed a μPAD with a detection limit of 1.30 mM for the chromogenic assay of nitrite and also took the color coordinates of HSV as the analysis parameter, in which a smartphone or digital camera were utilized (Ortiz-Gomez et al., 2016).

However, since the CDs have characteristics of non-toxicity, easy preparation, low cost, stable performance and are also scarce in spectrophotometric application, so this study will use the self-synthesized CDs to replace the traditional high-toxic reagent and develop a new, sensitive spectrophotometry for nitrite which can be assayed on routine instruments, and then, for people's healthy diet, the method will be applied to the research on the change of nitrite content in a wide concentration interval in some Chinese home cuisine, and do the preparation for further development of a new μPAD for assaying nitrite.

The related chemicals included sodium nitrite, CA, EDA, sulfanilic acid (SA), naphthalene ethylenediamine hydrochloride, sodium tetraborate, potassium ferricyanide and zinc acetate, and all these reagents are analysis-pure level (Chengdu, Colong chemical). Additionally, a HPD600 macroporous adsorption resin (Shandong, Honghua) column was also used, and water used in the experiment was ultrapure water (conductivity: 0.065 μS/cm).

The employed instruments in this experiment included a UV-1800-PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai, Mepuda), a HI-1290 pH meter (Italy, Hanna), a liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Thermo-Fisher TSQ Quantum Ultra), an AB204-N electronic analytical balance (Switzerland, Mettler-Toledo), a WP800T microwave oven (Chengdu, Galanz), a TP-3001 thermometer (Nanjing, Weiheng), a CM-230 Purified water apparatus (Chengdu, Eco) and a Tecnai G2-F20 type transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI, America).
A sodium nitrite standard solution of 1.0 g/L was used to prepare a series of working solutions, and a SA solution of 4 g/L was used as a diazotization reagent.

CA/EDA-CDs solution was utilized as a coupling reagent, its preparation process was that: 2 mL pure water and 5.0 g anhydrous citric acid were mixed in a 50-mL beaker, and then 5 mL anhydrous ethylenediamine was again added by drop-by-drop, in which it was heated continuously for 30 min under magnetic stirring until its solution was turned a brown-black viscous substance; after cooling to room temperature, the formed substance was dissolved with 500 mL water and stored at 4 °C in dark before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrum scanning and TEM characterizing for CA/EDA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In investigating the fluorescence spectrum, after the CA/EDA-CDs sol of 0.1 mL was diluted to 15 mL with water, the fluorescent scanning of the CDs sample in the 300–800 nm range was conducted at the optical passband of 1.5 × 10 nm; in UV–vis spectrum scanning, the CDs sol of 0.5 mL was diluted to 100 mL with water and use as the test sample; additionally, before TEM characterization, the CDs sample of 1 mL was diluted to 2 mL with water, and then ultrasonic dispersion was conducted for it at 40 °C and 180 W for 30 min.

The reaction principle and process of nitrite analyzed with the CDs-colorimetry is that nitrite (0.6 mL) protonated in acidic environment (20% HCl) is changed into nitronium ion (+NO), the latter with the SA reagent (0.4 mL, 4.0 g/L) cause a diazotized reaction (20 °C, 30 min), in which the formed intermediate is coupled with CA/EDA-CDs (0.4 mL) to generate a yellow azo product (440 nm). Finally, the absorbance of the azo product was used to calculate the nitrite concentration (in 10.0 mL total reaction volume).

According to general cooking methods in Chinese families, pure diced meat, peas and pakchoi were separately stir-fried by a cooking oil to make three kinds of common home cuisines. These home cuisines were divided into two portions and closed with the polyethylene film to cool naturally to room temperature, one portion was saved at room temperature and another portion was saved in 4 °C refrigerator. In making pickles, cabbages were washed, aired and then was put into a pickle jar.

Moreover, Chinese cabbage, lettuce, spinach and the green pepper pork (the ratio of pork and green pepper mass is 2:1) were fried with the cooking oil and cooled to the room temperature, and then these cuisines were also divided into two parts to save according to the above storage conditions. To prevent false positive appearing in testing nitrite, the edible salt without potassium iodate and the soy sauce without NaFe-EDTA were used in the cooking.

Before the test, samples of the home cuisines were pretreated by the reference method (GB-5009.33, 2016), namely grinded to homogenate liquid. The homogenate liquid of 5.0 g, saturated borax solution (50 g/L) of 12.5 mL and hot water of 150 mL (about 70 °C) were added into a 250-mL conical flask, which was heated in a water bath for 15 min and taken out to cool to room temperature. Then, the solution in the conical flask was moved quantitatively into a 250-mL volumetric flask, in which 5 mL potassium ferricyanide (106 g/L) and 5 mL zinc acetate solutions (220 g/L) were added to precipitate proteins and remove the sample color. The solution of the volumetric flask was diluted to the scale with water, and the superficial fat was removed after placed for 30 min. The supernatant was filtered by filter paper, in which the initial filtrate of 30 mL was discarded. The filtrate was used as the tested sample. Besides, we emphasize that the relevant ingredients used in this study were purchased from nearby supermarkets and vegetable markets, and were all fresh.

A sample of 4.0 mL was moved into a 10-mL graduated test-tube with a stopper and the sodium nitrite standard solution of different volumes (0.00, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.40, 0.50 and 0.60 mL) was added respectively into eight 10-mL test tubes, in which 0.2 mL SA (4 g/L) and 0.1 mL original solution of CDs were added respectively and were diluted to the calibration with water. This mixture solution in the tubes took place a diazo coupling for 15 min at room temperature, finally, the reacted solutions were decanted respectively into absorption cells with 1.0 cm optical-path to detect their absorbance at 440 nm wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of heating time and temperature in CDs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The reported methods for synthesizing CDs with citric acid and amine (Lim & Song, 2015) mainly were to utilize the microwave heating or the hydrothermal method under high temperature and pressure. The merits of the microwave method are fast in heating rate and short in time-consuming, but its demerits are that the synthesis process is difficult to control accurately and produces toxic smoke; the characteristic of the hydrothermal method is to easily control, but it requires a high pressure reactor and takes a long time. So, the study did not imitate fully two methods, and the optimization was conducted on conditions synthesizing CDs under ordinary-pressure hydrothermal heating.

Using ultraviolet–visible spectrum scanning at different heating-time, the synthesis process of CDs was evaluated and Fig. 1 was the obtained results, in which, calculation of the heating-time was started in finished the addition of ethylenediamine according to the operation steps in Section 2.2. Fig. 1a showed that the characteristic absorption (350 nm) of the synthetic CDs was enhanced gradually with the heating-time prolonged (5 → 30 min) and the temperature went up (110 → 165 °C), when heated for 30 min and the temperature reached 165 °C, the absorption signal has reached very high. So, in order to shorten the synthesis time, the heating-time and the temperature were selected 30 min and 165 °C, respectively. Enhancing of CDs’s characteristic absorption but the blue- or red-shifting no appear should be that the concentrated level of CDs was increased but its basic structure did not change (no wavelength-change) in the process of forming CDs through dehydration, polycondensation and carbonization with the heating-time prolonged and the temperature rose; so, the dilution degree of the test sample was the same in Fig. 1a, but the shown concentration of the CDs was different.

Fig. 1b is a series of UV–visible spectrums that are from the diazo intermediate product coupled with the CDs synthesized at different heating-time. It can be seen that if prolonged the heating-time in synthesizing the CDs, a new chromogenic product (445 nm) would be formed in the nitrite/SA/CA/EDA-CDs reaction, and its formation quantity would be also increased. The reason was also that the prolonging of the heating-time enhanced the poly-condensation of the synthesized CDs to increase the corresponding CDs concentration in the chromogenic reaction system.

Subsequently, the stability of the synthesized CDs was evaluated, in which the CDs were saved at 4 °C, and then its absorbance at 445 nm was measured once every 5 days. The experimental results manifested that the characteristic absorption of the CDs did not change within 60 days, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the azo product’s absorbance was <3.1%, which was obtained by adding 0.3 mg/L NO2 − in SA/CA/EDA-CDs reaction.

In the use of naphthalene ethylenediamine hydrochloride (NETH) as the coupling agent for analyzing NO2 − according to the GB method (GB-5009.33, 2016), the protonated NO2 − is first diazinated with the SA reagent, and then the formed diazonium reacts with NETH to generate a rose-red coupling compound. So, this experiment first confirmed the reaction between the diazonium with the CDs that replaces the NETH reagent. Obtained results are shown as Fig. 2 , which indicated that the CDs generated a yellow product (445 nm) after substituting for the NETH reagent.

To investigate whether being a coupling reaction between the CDs and the diazonium, comparison was conducted by changing the addition sequence of the CDs, SA reagent and nitrite in the mixture solution under the same experimental conditions. The curves in Fig. 2 indicated that, purple azo-products were generated when mixing in the order of NO2 −, SA and NETH (2 g/L), but the purple products did not formed if the addition order was reversed. Additionally, using the CDs to replace NETH, the emerging phenomenon was also similar, this indicated that the chromogenic reaction of the CDs is also a nitrogen coupling reaction, but only the product color is different.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different amines on CDs synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: Other experimental conditions remain unchanged, ammonia, hydrazine and urea containing amino were tried as passivation agents to synthesize different CDs (CA/NH3-CDs, CA/hydrazine-CDs, CA/urea-CDs) and appraise their effects on the coupling reaction. The obtained results are as shown in Fig. 3 A. It can be known that three kinds of CDs can react with the NO2 −/SA mixture to form the azo products, which have the maximum absorption at 410 nm and 435 nm, respectively. However, compared with the coupling product from CA/EDA-CDs, the sensitivity, namely the curve slope of CA/hydrazine-CDs reagent responding to NO2 − was low and its reagent blank was high, the linear response range of azo product from CA/NH3-CDs was narrow, and the reagent blank of CA/urea-CDs was too high. Therefore, CA/EDA-CDs were selected as the coupling agent of the CDs method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs formed by different EDA volumes on coupling reactions
ce:section.ce:para: The mass (2.0 g) of citric acid as the carbon source was fixed in synthesizing CA/EDA-CDs, and then effects of EDA’s addition amount was investigated with the coupling reaction, in which absorbance changes in the NO2 −/SA/CA/EDA-CDs reaction system were used to estimate the coupling reaction of the CDs formed under different EDA volumes. Curves in Fig. 3B indicated that when the adding volume of the EDA reagent was 1.5 mL, the formed CDs could maximize the subsequent azo products, so the ratio of 0.75 mL EDA and 1 g CA was selected to use for synthesizing the CDs in our assay method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectrum scanning and TEM characterizing for CA/EDA-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Molecular spectrum scanning and TEM-characterizing of the optimized CA/EDA-CDs were conducted with the fluorophotometer, spectrophotometer and the TEM apparatus, respectively.

The fluorescence spectrum of the CDs sol was shown in Fig. 3C, which indicates that at the maximum excitation wavelength of 370 nm, the CDs synthesized in the optimization can emit the characteristic fluorescence at 460 nm. The UV–vis spectrum scanning of the CDs sol was shown in Fig. 3D. The scanning curve indicated that after optimized, the maximum characteristic absorption of CA/EDA-CDs was still at 350 nm, which was the same with the test-result in Fig. 2. Besides, the liquid chromatography-mass spectrometer was also utilized to confirm the molecular structure and the molar mass of the chromogenic product in the NO2 −/SA/CA/EDA-CDs reaction, which are 1,2,3,5-tetrahydro-5-oxy-imidazolate [1,2-a] pyridin-7-acyl ethylenediamine and 405 g mol−1, as shown the inset in Fig. 3D. This indicated that the speculated structure is consistent with the molar mass and is reasonable.

Fig. 3E is four photographs of TEM characterization for the synthetic CA/EDA-CDs at different magnification, which indicated that the CDs are spherical, particle sizes are about 5 nm, and can be in a random dispersion state.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of common substances in samples on analyzing nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: Since this method will be intended for determination in food-samples in the 0.025–2.0 mg/L range, the nitrite standard solution of 0.2 mg/L was used as the test sample to investigate the influences of some ions (Added compound form: 1.0 g of NaCl, Na2SO4, Na2CO3, NaF, Na3PO4, NaNO3, NH4Cl, MgCl2, KCl, AlCl3 and CaCl2, and 20 mg/L CuSO4 and 8 mg/L FeCl3), which may coexist in some food samples. The added interference contents here are much higher than the allowable concentration in the real food samples.

The obtained results (Table S1) showed that after added these ions, the use of the NO2 −/SA/CA/EDA-CDs coloration reaction, the gotten addition recovery for the nitrite standard was in the range of 96–105%, which is permissible in requirements of the analytical chemistry, namely, these addition ions with the high concentration do not interfere with the low-concentration nitrite determination. But when the Fe3+ content was greater than 8 mg/L and Cu2+ content was greater than 20 mg/L, they would cause the effect. Therefore, if the Fe3+ content in the food samples is too high, NaOH can be added to remove it.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluations for reproducibility and linearity of CDs method
ce:section.ce:para:
Before the determination of food samples, reproducibility of the CDs-method was examined under the selected condition. Standard samples of nitrite (0.05, 0.2 mg/L) were used as test samples to consecutively measure for 11 times to obtain their RSD, in which the average absorbance of 0.05 mg/L nitrite was 0.040 ± 0.0021 (RSD < 5.0%) and that of 0.2 mg/L nitrite was 0.158 ± 0.0050 (RSD < 3.2%). The data of RSD manifested that the new method is good in the repeatability. Then, the linear response of the method was investigated, the related results are shown as shown in Fig. 4 . The range of the linear response is from 0.025 mg/L to 2.0 mg/L, in which its linear correlation is good (A = 0.658c + 0.027, r = 0.9990). Through calculation, the detection limit of this method is 9.6 μg/L (3SD/k). According to the kinetic curves inserted in Fig. 4, the chromogenic time of the CDs-method was selected for 15 min. Additionally, in order to compare the measured results, the GB method (GB-5009.33, , 2016) was also used as a reference method, which the investigation of its linearity was also conducted and the gotten result indicated the linear response of the GB method is also satisfied (A = 1.072c + 0.0051, r = 0.9993).
Comparison with the published results for determining nitrite

In Table 1 , results of several methods related to electrochemistry, fluorescence, chemiluminescence, colorimetry and μPADs were collected. The criteria used for comparison are detection limit (LOD), linear range, etc. As can be inferred from the dynamic linear ranges in Table 1, the proposed method has a wider linear range and a higher sensitivity, and its characteristic is also satisfactory, but in terms of the reagent dosage in the measure each time, the assay cost of the CDs-method may be slightly higher than that of the μPADs method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of nitrite content in Chinese home cooking
ce:section.ce:para: The photos in Fig. 5 , from left to right, are the stir-fried diced meat and fried peas, pickles and fried cabbage; the temperature difference between night and day was in the range of 18–29 °C in doing experiment. After treated these foods by the process in Section 2.5, the CDs-method were used to monitor the changes of the nitrite content in the test samples over time in two preservation environments.

It can be seen from the curves in Fig. 5, the nitrite content in the pickles, peas and pakchoi vegetables in the refrigerator (4 °C) for 5 days have not exceed the recommended value (20 mg/kg), in which, the nitrite content of the pakchoi did not change much in 3 days, but the nitrite content in the fried pakchoi at the room temperature had exceeded the recommended value after one day (24 h), this indicated that these vegetables should be eaten up in a day if being at the room temperature. Besides, the curve trend in Fig. 5 also showed that at room temperature, the high-content nitrate in leafy vegetables will be converted easily to nitrite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature and time on nitrite content in leafy vegetables
ce:section.ce:para: The above experiment proved that the nitrite content in leafy vegetables varies greatly, therefore, the experiment further investigated the effect of preservation temperature and time on the nitrite content in four stir-fried pure leafy vegetables of Chinese cabbage (c), spinach (s), lettuce (l) and shredded green pepper pork (g) (photos from left to right in Fig. 6 a), in which the test conditions was the same with that above-mentioned. As can be seen from curves in Fig. 6a, the nitrite content in the four dishes kept at 4 °C for three days indicated little change trend, all of which were lower than the recommended value. Although there was slight change in the apparent color of these dishes, but no peculiar smell was produced. Three days later, except the fried Chinese cabbage, the nitrite contents in the other three fried vegetables rose rapidly and exceeded the recommended value.

From Fig. 6b, it can be known that, the apparent colors of the four fried vegetables (c, g, l, s) stored at the room temperature (fluctuate: 17–26 °C) occurred the change and produced the spoiled smell after a day (24 h); on the second day (fluctuate: 14–18 °C), the colors were turned yellow and the vegetables had foul smell.

Additionally, the four curves in Fig. 6b indicated that the nitrite contents in the four fried vegetables were increased rapidly from the first day, but decreased rapidly from the second day. This manifested that, the nitrite content in the fried vegetables is related to microbial growth, due to the rapid propagation of microorganisms at room temperature, nitrate in the vegetables was transformed into nitrite, further, under the action of nitrite reductase, the microbial growth resulted in the food spoilage and the rapid degrade of nitrite, so showing a decrease in nitrite content on the second day (Zhang, Lin, & Zhu, 2008).

In order to confirm the accuracy of the results assayed by the CDs-method, synchronously, the GB method (GB-5009.33, , 2016) was also utilized to analyze the nitrite in these vegetables every day, and all obtained results were listed in the supplementary material (Table S2). The comparison between the two methods indicated that the results of the CDs-method were accurate, and the deviation values contraposing the data of the GB method were very small.

In the research, under the ordinary-pressure hydrothermal condition, the CDs synthesized by citric acid and amine can be used as a diazonium coupling chromogenic reagent to assay the trace-level nitrite, in which the linearity of the CDs-method in 0.025–2.0 mg/L range is good, and common ions in foods do not produce the interference. The merits of CDs as the coupling agent are non-toxic, low cost, easy to prepare, and can be stored for a long time. Analysis results of the CDs-method are consistent with the standard reference. Finally, using the CDs-method to continuously monitor the nitrite contents in some Chinese home cooking, we found that the nitrite content of the fried leafy vegetables stored in 4 °C refrigerator within 72 h do not exceed the recommended value and all can eat them with reassurance, but the storage in the room temperature over 24 h, it will exceed the recommended value and become inedible.

Yong-Sheng Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Original manuscript writing, Revising manuscript. Chun-Lin Zhao: Experiments, Preparation and Assay of Chinese home cooking, CDs synthesis, Writing-Original draft preparation. Bao-Lin Li: Experiments, CDs synthesis. Xiu-Feng Gao: CDs Separation, Chromatography, Writing-Reviewing and Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127151.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
